exemplar.title: How does beach exposure affect the abundance of Barnacles (Semibalanus balanoides) in Dale, Wales?

detailedExemplarData.title: How does beach exposure affect the abundance of Barnacles (Semibalanus balanoides) in Dale, Wales?

subject.title: Environmental systems and societies (ESS - Old)
subject.description: IB Environmental Systems and Societies (ESS) examines environmental challenges through an interdisciplinary approach combining natural sciences with social and economic perspectives. Students study ecosystems, biodiversity, resource management, pollution, climate change, and sustainability using systems thinking and collaborative investigation. The course develops environmental literacy and critical thinking skills essential for understanding and addressing complex environmental issues facing our planet.

files.fileName: ESS - Sofia Santecchia IA final draft (1).pdf

strengths: Report is well structured with consistent headings and navigation aids
Effective use of scientific terminology in most sections
weaknesses: Occasional verbosity and typographical errors reduce concision
Abrupt data formatting and appendix references hinder coherence at times
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Structure is consistently strong (band 3), but issues in concision and occasional lapses in logical flow align with band 2 descriptors. Thus, a mark of 2 reflects mainly logical, wellâorganized communication with minor disruptions.

strengths: Precisely formulated, focused research question specifying IV, DV, and location
Clear operational definitions facilitating quantitative investigation
Strong linkage of climate-driven wave action to barnacle abundance
weaknesses: Occasional digression into broader storm contexts without quantitative linkage
Speculative claim about historical barnacle similarity lacking supporting data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs research question is exceptionally clear and focused (band 6), and the context and connections are well articulated (band 5 each) with minor digressions and one speculative link. On balance, this criterion merits a mark of 5, reflecting very strong but not flawless performance.

strengths: Comprehensive, repeatable field protocol with clear transect and quadrat details
Wellâjustified sampling strategy using cumulative mean method
Thorough risk assessment with controls aligned to IB policy
weaknesses: Omission of salinity as a control variable
Limited detail on Ballantine scale calibration and percentageâcover conversion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Method design and sampling rationale align with topâband descriptors (band 5), while risk and ethical considerations fully meet topâband (band 6). Overall, a mark of 5 reflects a highly appropriate and largely sufficient plan with only minor areas for greater detail.

strengths: Evaluation relates findings to environmental issue and acknowledges alternative explanations
Clear identification of methodological strengths and limitations
Meaningful improvement suggestions (seasonal repeats, image analysis, broader gradients)
weaknesses: Broader ecosystem and quantitative significance implications are only briefly touched upon
Improvements lack full justification against all identified limitations
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Conclusion and method evaluation meet band 4 criteria (solid evaluation with some omissions), while improvements correspond to band 3 (meaningful but not comprehensive). Averaging these strands yields a final mark of 4.

strengths: Complete presentation of raw and summary data in tables and graphs
Correct and coherent statistical analysis including t-test and summary statistics
Conclusion directly answers the research question and is supported by data
weaknesses: Graphs lack full axis units and error bars
No discussion of effect size or test assumptions
Vague wording in the conclusion regarding magnitude of difference
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data presentation, analysis, and conclusion consistently align with band 5 descriptors: appropriate and accurate but with minor omissions in formatting and depth. Therefore, a score of 5 accurately reflects this highâquality work.

strengths: Application proposal clearly derives from study findings
Justification connects living shoreline design to exposure reduction
Balanced appraisal of solution strengths and weaknesses
weaknesses: Socioeconomic feasibility and longâterm monitoring requirements not addressed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Both application proposal and solution evaluation fully meet the highest band descriptors. Despite one omitted consideration, the overall performance justifies the maximum mark of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: Scientific terminology (e.g., Ballantine scale, cumulative mean, t-test) is generally accurate and used consistently. Nevertheless, occasional typographical errors and verbosity (long sentences, repeated phrases) reduce concision, placing the work in the middle band where either terminology or concision is fully met but not both.

The student relates the findings to intensified wave action and briefly considers rival explanations, weighing their likelihood. However, the broader implications for ecosystem management and climate change are only touched upon, and quantitative significance is not explored. Thus, the evaluation is good with omissions, matching band 3-4 at the upper mark.

The student rejects the null hypothesis using the calculated t statistic and integrates numerical results with graphical patterns. The conclusion explicitly answers the research question and is fully supported by the data set. Minor wording issues ("more than 10 units") do not undermine validity, but slight speculation about interference without follow-up prevents a perfect 6.

Means, standard deviations and an independent-samples t-test with correct df and critical value are calculated. Patterns in both mean and spread are identified. Further exploration of effect size or assumptions (normality) is absent; however, the analysis is otherwise complete and correct, satisfying the top-band criteria at level 5.

Proposed improvements (seasonal repetition, digital image analysis, broader exposure gradient, Spearmanâs rank) address key weaknesses and extend the study. Yet, the suggestions are briefly justified and do not fully tackle all major limitations (e.g., replication at additional sites within each exposure category). This aligns with meaningful rather than significant modifications.

A repeatable protocol (transect length, quadrat size, sample size determination, variable controls) is provided. Still, randomisation within systematic placement and exact percentage-cover estimation method are only briefly stated, creating small uncertainties about sufficiency for all relevant data. Therefore, the method is appropriate and largely sufficient, fitting the top band but not perfect, so 5.

Explicit links are drawn between rising wave energy, differential exposure of the two beaches and predicted barnacle abundance. Alternative explanations (substrate, predators) are acknowledged, showing understanding of causality. However, the explanation of why the historical similarity in abundance is expected is brief and partially speculative, leaving some omissions. Hence a comprehensive but not exhaustive explanation merits 5.

The student formulates a precise, single-sentence question that specifies the independent variable (beach exposure), the dependent variable (abundance of Semibalanus balanoides), and the geographical location (Dale, Wales). The wording is unambiguous and lends itself directly to quantitative investigation with clear operational definitions, demonstrating both coherence and focus. No peripheral or compound elements detract from the clarity, so the highest band is warranted.

A balanced appraisal of the solutionâs strengths (habitat creation, self-repair, cost) and weaknesses (site suitability, time to establish, invasive species risk, long-term efficacy) is offered. The discussion is comprehensive, addressing ecological, practical and temporal aspects, fulfilling the descriptor for full evaluation.

The report follows the prescribed IA structure with consistent headings, tables, figures and appendices, facilitating navigation. Minor layout glitches (table breaks, figure captions) do not disrupt overall organisation, so the work is well-structured throughout.

Strengths (systematic sampling, control variables, preliminary study) and weaknesses/limitations (human counting error, only two beaches, time constraints) are clearly described with explanation of their influence on reliability and validity. Some factors (e.g., quadrat size, Ballantine accuracy) are not considered, so the discussion is sound but not exhaustive.

The student discusses climate-driven increases in wave action, barnacle ecological roles, and the broader implications for intertidal biodiversity, citing several sources. While the narrative is detailed, the discussion occasionally digresses (e.g., mention of hurricanes) and some claims lack quantitative support, so it is strong but not flawless. This aligns with the upper band, but minor gaps justify 5 rather than 6.

The student explains choosing systematic sampling along a 15 m transect to ensure full spatial coverage and reduce bias; the cumulative-mean approach to justify n = 40 further supports the choice. The rationale is clear though not deeply contrasted with alternative strategies (e.g., stratified), so the justification is solid but not exemplary.

Ethical compliance with IB policy is explicitly referenced and justified; the risk table identifies a range of realistic hazards, assigns severity/likelihood scores, and shows reduced risk after controls. Both animal-welfare and safety considerations are treated thoroughly, meeting all descriptors for a comprehensive treatment.

All relevant raw, processed and summary data are tabulated; bar chart with error bars and cumulative-mean graphs are appropriate. Minor formatting problems (partial table truncation, axis labels small) and omission of full units on graphs prevent a flawless score, but overall coverage and accuracy place the work firmly in the top band at 5.

The proposal of living shorelines is clearly connected to reducing wave energy and thus protecting barnacle abundance; ecological mechanisms and literature support are provided. The justification is explicitly grounded in the studyâs findings about exposure, meeting the highest band for this strand.

The argument flows logically from context to planning, results and evaluation; however, some sections (raw data formatting, abrupt appendix references) momentarily hinder reader follow-through. These minor coherence issues align with a mainly logical presentation that is occasionally difficult to follow.

annotations.comment: The research question is exceptionally well defined, specifying the independent and dependent variables and the precise location, which enhances clarity and focus.
While the broader climate context is informative, the discussion on hurricanes and storms digresses from the local intertidal system and lacks quantitative linkage to barnacle abundance.
Figure 1 provides valuable background but should explicitly explain how North Atlantic Oscillation trends translate into local wave exposure differences at Dale.
The claim that geographical similarity implies historical barnacle abundance requires supporting historical data; this link is speculative without quantitative evidence.
The hypothesis clearly predicts that increased exposure reduces S. balanoides abundance, linking variables directly and facilitating statistical testing.
The variable table identifies key inaccuracies but should reference the Ballantine exposure scale details or calibration protocol to ensure reproducibility.
The control variable table is comprehensive but omits salinity, which can affect barnacle settlement; consider adding it.
The preliminary SACFOR classification lacks justification for its conversion to percentage cover; clarify scale selection and conversion methodology.
The cumulative mean method for sampleâsize determination is well applied, demonstrating sound planning for data reliability.
The systematic sampling is well described; however, referencing stratified sampling alternatives would strengthen justification by comparison.
Ethical compliance is clearly stated with IB policy reference, demonstrating thorough consideration for minimal impact on S. balanoides.
The risk assessment table is detailed with severity and likelihood scores and postâcontrol reductions, meeting topâband planning criteria.
The summary table of means and standard deviations omits units for percentage cover; include â%â to enhance clarity.
Including error bars on the mean comparison graph would visually convey variability and strengthen data presentation.
Hypotheses are correctly formalized using null and alternative constructs with directionality, supporting rigorous statistical testing.
Clarify whether a oneâtailed tâtest is fully justified by the hypothesis and ensure pâvalue interpretation aligns with test directionality.
The conclusion effectively integrates statistical results but should replace vague âmore than 10 unitsâ with the precise percentage difference of 11%.
Speculative comments on initial graph variability require supporting analysis (e.g., breakâpoint tests) rather than assumption of negligible impact.
Discussion correctly acknowledges alternate causal factors, but lacks integration of quantified effect sizes or implications for management.
Identification of human counting error and microhabitat variation demonstrates a sound evaluation of methodological weaknesses.
Proposal to use digital image analysis is promising; strengthen by citing studies quantifying its accuracy improvement over manual counts.
The living shoreline application is well linked to findings on exposure, explicitly justifying how it mitigates waveâinduced barnacle loss.
Discussion of living shoreline limitations omits socioeconomic feasibility and longâterm monitoring requirements; expand evaluation in this context.
The title succinctly conveys the study focus, species and location, promoting immediate understanding.
Including the word count in the main author block distracts; consider moving it to a cover-page footnote or header for cleaner presentation.
Consider numbering or subheading the âEnvironmental issue and connectionâ section for improved navigation and clearer structure.
